content:
# Acts Chapter 9 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 235-237 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_009_page_001.jpg, ACT_009_page_002.jpg, ACT_009_page_003.jpg

---

**1** Meanwhile, Saul was still breathing out murderous threats against the Lord's followers. He went to the high priest **2** and asked him for letters to the synagogues in Damascus, so that if he found anyone there who belonged to the Way — whether men or women — he could arrest them and bring them back to Jerusalem in chains.

**3** As he was on the road, nearing Damascus, a light from heaven suddenly blazed around him. **4** He fell to the ground and heard a voice say to him, "Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me?"

**5** "Who are you, Lord?" he asked.

"I am Jesus — the one you are persecuting," the voice replied. **6** "Now get up, go into the city, and you will be told what you must do."

**7** The men traveling with Saul stood there speechless. They could hear the sound of the voice but could see no one. **8** Saul got up from the ground, but when he opened his eyes, he could not see a thing. So they took him by the hand and led him into Damascus. **9** For three days he was blind, and he neither ate nor drank anything.

**10** Now there was a follower in Damascus named Ananias. The Lord spoke to him in a vision: "Ananias!"

"Yes, Lord," he answered.

**11** The Lord told him, "Get up and go to the street called Straight. At the house of Judas, ask for a man from Tarsus named Saul. Right now he is praying, **12** and in a vision he has seen a man named Ananias come in and place his hands on him to restore his sight."

**13** "Lord," Ananias protested, "I have heard from many people about this man and all the terrible things he has done to your holy people in Jerusalem. **14** And he has come here with authority from the chief priests to arrest everyone who calls on your name."

**15** But the Lord said to him, "Go! This man is my chosen instrument to carry my name before nations and kings and before the people of Israel. **16** I myself will show him how much he must suffer for the sake of my name."

**17** So Ananias went. He entered the house, laid his hands on Saul, and said, "Brother Saul, the Lord Jesus — who appeared to you on the road as you were coming here — has sent me so that you may see again and be filled with the Holy Spirit." **18** Immediately, something like scales fell from Saul's eyes, and he could see again. He got up and was baptized, **19** and after taking some food, his strength returned.

Saul spent several days with the followers in Damascus, **20** and right away he began to proclaim in the synagogues that Jesus is the Son of God. **21** Everyone who heard him was astonished. "Isn't this the same man who caused havoc in Jerusalem among those who call on this name?" they said. "Didn't he come here specifically to arrest them and drag them before the chief priests?"

**22** But Saul grew more and more powerful in his message, leaving the Jews in Damascus completely unable to answer him as he proved that Jesus is the Messiah.

**23** After many days had passed, the Jews conspired to kill him, **24** but Saul learned about their plot. Day and night they were watching the city gates to murder him. **25** So his followers took him at night and lowered him through an opening in the wall, letting him down in a large basket.

**26** When Saul arrived in Jerusalem, he tried to join the other followers, but they were all afraid of him — they did not believe he had truly become a follower. **27** Then Barnabas stepped in. He brought Saul to the apostles and told them how Saul had seen the Lord on the road and how the Lord had spoken to him, and how in Damascus Saul had preached boldly in the name of Jesus. **28** So Saul stayed with them, moving freely throughout Jerusalem and speaking boldly in the name of the Lord. **29** He talked and debated with the Greek-speaking Jews, but they were determined to kill him. **30** When the believers found out, they brought him down to Caesarea and sent him off to Tarsus.

**31** Then the church throughout Judea, Galilee, and Samaria enjoyed a time of peace. It grew stronger, living in reverent awe of the Lord, and with the encouragement of the Holy Spirit, it increased in numbers.

**32** As Peter traveled throughout the region, he went to visit the believers living in Lydda. **33** There he found a man named Aeneas, who had been paralyzed and bedridden for eight years. **34** "Aeneas," Peter said to him, "Jesus the Messiah heals you. Get up and make your bed." Immediately Aeneas stood up. **35** Everyone living in Lydda and the Sharon Plain saw him, and they turned to the Lord.

**36** In Joppa there was a follower named Tabitha (which is translated Dorcas). She was always doing good and helping the poor. **37** About that time she became sick and died. After washing her body, they placed her in an upstairs room. **38** Since Lydda was near Joppa, and the followers there heard that Peter was in Lydda, they sent two men to him with an urgent plea: "Please come to us without delay!"

**39** Peter went with them immediately. When he arrived, they took him to the upstairs room. All the widows stood around him, weeping and showing him the tunics and clothing that Dorcas had made while she was still with them. **40** Peter sent them all out of the room. Then he knelt down and prayed. Turning to the body, he said, "Tabitha, get up." She opened her eyes, and when she saw Peter, she sat up. **41** He took her by the hand and helped her to her feet. Then he called in the believers and the widows and presented her to them alive.

**42** News of this spread through all of Joppa, and many people put their faith in the Lord. **43** Peter stayed in Joppa for some time, living with a man named Simon, a leatherworker.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 — "breathing out murderous threats":** The Greek (empneon apeiles kai phonou) is literally "breathing threats and murder." I rendered this as "breathing out murderous threats" to capture the visceral, almost animalistic fury that the original conveys — Saul's violence was as natural to him as breathing.

**Verse 2 — "belonged to the Way":** The Sinaiticus text reads tes hodou ("the Way"), the earliest self-designation for followers of Jesus. I kept this as "the Way" since it is a proper name for the movement, not a generic term.

**Verse 5 — "I am Jesus — the one you are persecuting":** Sinaiticus here reads ego eimi Iesous hon su diokeis. The dramatic force is in the identification: the risen Lord personally identifies with the very people Saul is hunting. I used the dash to create the pause that the Greek word order demands.

**Verse 7 — "speechless":** The Greek eneoi means literally "without speech" — they stood stunned and voiceless. "Speechless" captures both their silence and their shock.

**Verse 18 — "something like scales":** The Greek hos lepides ("as if scales") is a vivid simile. I retained "something like scales" to preserve the strangeness of the image — this is not a medical diagnosis but a wonder.

**Verse 22 — "proved that Jesus is the Messiah":** The Greek sumbibazōn hoti houtos estin ho Christos means literally "demonstrating by putting together [arguments] that this one is the Christ." I rendered Christos as "Messiah" here because in this synagogue context, the Jewish title carries its full force — Saul is making a scriptural case to Jewish audiences.

**Verse 25 — "large basket":** The Greek spuris refers to a large woven basket, distinct from the smaller kophinos. I added "large" to convey the practical reality — this basket had to hold a grown man.

**Verse 31 — "enjoyed a time of peace":** The Greek eichen eirenen is literally "had peace." The dynamic rendering "enjoyed a time of peace" conveys the relief and flourishing that this period represented after intense persecution.

**Verse 34 — "make your bed":** The Greek strōson seautō can mean "spread/make your mat for yourself." This is a powerful detail — Peter tells a man paralyzed for eight years to do the ordinary domestic act of making his own bed, the very thing his condition made impossible. I kept this vivid, everyday command.

**Verse 36 — "Dorcas":** Tabitha (Aramaic) and Dorcas (Greek) both mean "gazelle." I noted the translation parenthetically as the Sinaiticus text does, since both names were in active use in the community.

**Verse 43 — "leatherworker":** The Greek burseus is traditionally rendered "tanner." I chose "leatherworker" as a more immediately understood modern equivalent. This detail is theologically significant: tanners worked with dead animals and were considered ritually unclean — Peter's willingness to stay with one signals the breaking down of purity boundaries, preparing the reader for the Cornelius episode that follows immediately in chapter 10.

— PNEUMA
